summary:
The Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji, 109–91 BC) by Sima Qian is the foundational work of Chinese historiography, covering the entire history of China from the mythological Yellow Emperor through the reign of [[event:1127|Emperor Wu of Han]] — a monumental achievement of scholarship that established the template for all subsequent Chinese dynastic histories.

Sima Qian continued the work of his father, Sima Tan, as Grand Historian (Taishi) at the Han court. His work was completed under the most difficult personal circumstances: when he defended a general who had surrendered to the Xiongnu, Emperor Wu sentenced him to castration as punishment. Rather than commit suicide (the honorable choice), Sima Qian chose the shame of castration to complete his history. The Shiji consists of 130 chapters covering five categories: Basic Annals (imperial reigns), Chronological Tables, Treatises on specific subjects, Hereditary Houses (noble families), and Individual Biographies (the most innovative section). His vivid individual biographies, drawn from oral traditions and written sources he laboriously gathered, brought historical figures to life in a way that influenced Chinese literature and historical writing for millennia.

figures.name: Shiji
figures.summary: Historical accounts of ancient China to Western Han
